title: Acute Delirium in a Hospitalized Older Adult: Recognize It, Find the Cause
subtitle: An 82-year-old woman on POD2 after hip-fracture repair develops sudden, fluctuating confusion and inattention. Family says she was sharp at home. The nurse must recognize delirium, hunt for reversible causes, and manage it without restraints or benzodiazepines.

patient.name: Eleanor Pruitt
patient.admitDate: POD2 after left hip-fracture repair. New acute, fluctuating confusion with inattention overnight. Baseline (per daughter) alert and oriented, lives independently. On scheduled oxycodone + PRN diphenhydramine for sleep; has not voided well; low-grade fever.

vitals.temp: Hip pain; not voided; on opioid + diphenhydramine

labs.v: Sharp and independent at home per family.
labs.r: This is an ACUTE change, not dementia.

nursesNote: Eleanor Pruitt, 82, POD2 hip repair with new overnight confusion that comes and goes, plus inattention and disorganized thinking. Baseline alert/oriented per daughter. Low-grade fever with a positive UA, 600 mL bladder retention, Na 129, SpO2 93%, and deliriogenic meds (opioid, diphenhydramine, PRN lorazepam). CAM positive. Plan: confirm delirium, treat reversible causes, non-pharmacologic management, avoid restraints/benzodiazepines.
providerOrders: Confirm acute mental-status change with CAM; rule out reversible causes
Treat UTI per orders; relieve urinary retention; supplemental O2 for hypoxia
Review and minimize deliriogenic meds (diphenhydramine, PRN benzodiazepine)
Non-pharmacologic delirium bundle: reorient, sensory aids, sleep-wake support, mobilize
Reserve low-dose antipsychotic for severe agitation threatening safety only; reassess mental status each shift

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What points to acute delirium rather than baseline dementia?

opts.text: Confusion of sudden onset that FLUCTUATES over hours suggests delirium
Inattention — can't sustain or follow attention — is a core feature
Disorganized thinking and altered consciousness support the diagnosis
Family report that she was sharp at home means this is an ACUTE change
A gradual, stable memory decline over years with intact attention is delirium
Look for a reversible trigger: infection, drugs, hypoxia, pain, retention

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: How do you confirm and interpret it?

opts.text: CAM: acute/fluctuating + inattention + (disorganized thinking OR altered LOC) = positive
Delirium is often the FIRST or only sign of a new medical problem in elders
Quiet, withdrawn (hypoactive) delirium is common and easily missed
Anticholinergics, benzodiazepines, and opioids can precipitate delirium
New confusion in an older adult is normal aging and needs no workup
Urinary retention, constipation, and hypoxia are reversible contributors
Recognize the acute change and confirm delirium with CAM
Find and treat reversible causes (UTI, retention, hypoxia, meds)
Provide non-pharmacologic management and a safe environment
Reserve low-dose medication only for severe, unsafe agitation
Reassess mental status and document the response
Acute onset over hours-to-days with a fluctuating course
Prominent inattention and altered level of consciousness
Often reversible once the underlying cause is treated
Gradual, progressive memory loss over years; attention preserved early
Chronic and largely irreversible; consciousness usually normal
Weeks of low mood and anhedonia with frequent "I don't know" answers
Sad affect with sleep/appetite change; attention largely intact

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Assess and confirm the acute mental-status change (CAM)
Address reversible causes: treat UTI, relieve retention, give O2, manage pain, review meds
Reorient; provide glasses/hearing aids, family presence, day-night cues
Keep her safe with least-restrictive measures; avoid restraints and benzodiazepines
Reassess frequently and document the trend
After the UTI is treated and retention relieved, her attention returns
She recognizes family and follows simple commands again
Sleep-wake cycle normalizes and agitation settles without sedatives
Mental status returns to her baseline over the next few days
She is placed in wrist restraints and becomes more agitated
A benzodiazepine is given for agitation and she grows more confused
The confusion is dismissed as dementia and the UTI goes untreated
She stays in bed for days, deconditions, and develops a pressure injury

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Management of Care, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
